narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including scoring a solid 36 on your LSAT and planning to attend full-time, but unfortunately, our admissions model has predicted a 73.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, family details, and exam outcomes, where the goal is to identify those most likely to pass the bar right away. The biggest factor pushing the model toward this concerning prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is well below the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile—this low standing signaled a higher risk of bar struggles compared to most applicants. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than average, added to that concern by suggesting early academic challenges that carried over. On a more positive note, your LSAT score, very close to the dataset average, helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success, showing your potential in standardized testing. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, slightly below the typical 3.21, nudged things a bit toward failure risk, though not as strongly as your decile rankings. Meanwhile, your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans offered minor boosts toward passing, aligning with common profiles of successful students. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students mainly because of those lower decile rankings, which outweighed your strengths in LSAT and background. While this is disappointing, it's not the end of the road—consider retaking courses or boosting your GPA through post-bac programs to improve those decile positions, as schools often re-rank based on stronger performances. You might also target schools with more flexible admissions or preparatory bar support tailored for full-time students like you. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these key academic areas, as many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing on consistent improvement. Your determination as a white male from a supportive background positions you well for that comeback. Keep pushing forward—you've got this.
